utterances: You know, very grudgingly, though, and maybe as a part of strategy.
They also adopted Gandhi as an icon.
They had to, rather.
Otherwise it is very difficult to survive politically.
Prime Minister Modi is headlining events for Gandhi's 150th birthday.
The Mahatma's signature round spectacles are the logo for Modi's Clean India campaign - something Gandhi inspired.
On weekends in Delhi, crowds pay their respects at the spot where Gandhi was cremated.
Among them is Harsh Parikh (ph), age 17.
He and his friends debate the freedom leader's tactics.
When someone says, like, oh, he was great, there's always some criticism.
Like, no, he could have got us freedom way before and all that.
What Harsh and his friends are discussing is at the very heart of Gandhian philosophy, says biographer Guha.
His absolute insistence on nonviolence, which young men see as pussyfooted and weak-kneed and, dare I say it, feminine and hence not macho enough.

Gandhi's restraint can look naive to countries struggling to confront terrorism; so can some of his other ideas in today's booming globalized India, like his swadeshi movement, emphasizing small-scale local industries.
(SOUNDBITE OF WHEEL SPINNING)
A 76-year-old man sits with a spinning wheel at the other Gandhi memorial across town.
There are so many across India.
Why do you come here?
Why do you do this?
(Laughter) Because I'm very much impressed with Gandhi and his work.
As a tribute to him, every Friday I come here.
Musaddilal Gupta (ph) is a retired civil servant who escapes here for an hour a week to spin his own thread as Gandhi taught.
His principle are so strong.
You may or may not accept it.
But the day will come when (speaking Hindi).
The day will come when this palace of cards will fall,"" he says, ""and Gandhian principles will remain."" Lauren Frayer, NPR News, New Delhi.
(SOUNDBITE OF DEEPAK RAM'S ""MOM"")
Europe is being hit by a heat wave.
Spain, Germany and Italy have all seen record temperatures.
And in France, the government has had to postpone school exams and limit the number of cars in the city of Paris.
Jake Cigainero sent us this postcard from Paris.
Temperatures in Paris are hovering around the mid-90s, and this in a city where hardly anyone has air conditioning in their homes.
(Laughter).
(SOUNDBITE OF WATER SPLASHING)
So Parisians are jumping into the water wherever they can.
In northeast Paris, children splash around in a fountain.
Florian Bulliard brought his 3- and 6-year-old kids to help them cool off.
He says it's too hot for the kids to do anything else.
I come from a small town in a mountain, so I'm not used to have it this hot.
So it's very difficult for us.
His family has even thought about going back to the mountains to escape the heat wave.
The French government takes heat waves very seriously after 15,000 people died in a record hot spell in 2003.
So Paris now has a smartphone application that points Parisians to places where they can cool off.
The city has opened cooling centers around Paris, and there's also a call center that checks on the elderly, who are particularly at risk.
(SOUNDBITE OF WATER SPLASHING)
Thank you.
What I'm saying is that, the two weeks of festivities that have bloomed around a two-minute horse race every first Saturday in May, is Louisville's equivalent of the New Orleans Mardi Gras.

And though I doubt that there still are hotel rooms or unsold seats on scheduled flights to Louisville to be had during the first week in May, this transplanted New Yorker is urging all who can, and all who will to get here when you can.
And has a certain hotel chain likes to say: We'll leave a light on for ya.
Betty Baye is a columnist for the Courier Journal in Louisville, Kentucky.
This is DAY TO DAY.
I'm Madeleine Brand.
And I'm Luke Burbank.

I did cleaning, cooking, ironing - everything there is to do in a house.
More than one million women immigrate to the Gulf Region to work.
Most are from Southeast Asia, and they don't really have any rights.
First, in this country, gas prices are surging again.
According to AAA, the average price per gallon across the country is a little more than $2.50 a gallon.
Yeah, and it's even more around here, Madeleine.
It's like $3 a gallon, which is why I've been driving on fumes for the last week.
I don't know if I can wade it out.
Frankly, though, NPR's Mike Pesca is here to help explain why gas prices are so high.
Philip Verleger, oil expert, economist, self-styled realist, looks at his local filling station, looks at the daily newspaper and doesn't make the same connection that everyone else is making.
I've worked for Greenspan for a year in the government.
I - Greenspan would say, well, there's a fair premium in the price of oil.
There's no fair premium in the price of oil.

The former Fed chairman, Alan Greenspan, and just about everybody who has ever written a summation of rising oil prices, can't help but think, hey, oil comes from troubled regions - that must affect prices.
The latest variation of this thought comes every time Hugo Chavez sniffs the sulfurous emanations of President Bush.
Oil watchers wonder a little bit about the stability of the Venezuelan leader and the huge oil fields he lords over.
Hugo Chavez is a compelling figure, and certainly, someone for an oil trader to keep an eye on.
But Lisa Margonelli, author of ""Oil on the Brain,"" says gas prices are the result of the simplest calculation of all - demand is so much greater than supply.
It's not like Econ 101, where when widget prices get high, people stop using widgets.
We just keep right on driving.
The world's demand for oil knows fewer limits, just as the capacity to produce oil is hamstrung, says Philip Verleger.
It seems like every morning, you wake up, and there is yet another problem at a refinery.
Refineries are pumping out oil at something like 96 percent of their capacity, and this is the season when they retool in order to switch blends.
That always results in higher gas prices.
The oil companies say they'd love to build more refineries.
But most experts will tell you that's not the case.
They're making money hand over fist with things as they are.
So it's a kind of gridlock, and that means higher prices.
The refineries were hit hard by hurricanes, Katrina and Rita, in 2005.
A huge fire swept through the BP refinery in Texas City that year.
But smaller mishaps have also beset refineries.
One problem is that the oil companies bought out and laid off workers when gas prices were low in the '90s.
And Verleger says, they're feeling the pinch today.
Now, that with everything's booming, they can't find the chemical engineers.
And they have to pay over $100,000 to hire these kids straight out of college.
And if you think gas prices are bad now, Verleger says, it could get a lot worse.
You have no idea how high it could get if a couple more refineries were to go down, and if the EPA did not relax environmental standards.
And so consumers and consumer reporters are obsessed with rising prices, as ""Dateline NBC"" recently noticed.
